coredata.dc:title: Terbium (III)-referenced N-doped carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescent sensing of mercury (II) in seafood
coredata.dc:creator: He, Xie
Han, Yong
Luo, Xueli
Yang, Weixia
Li, Chunhua
Tang, Wenzhi
Yue, Tianli
Li, Zhonghong
coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric fluorescence (FL) sensor was fabricated by coordinating 2, 6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) sensitized Tb3+ (Tb-DPA) with NH2 and COOH on the surface of the N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for detecting Hg2+ in seafood. The sensor exhibited two FL emissions at 436 nm (N-CDs) as the response signal and at 543 nm (Tb-DPA) as the reference signal when excited at 290 nm. After adding Hg2+, the FL emission at 436 nm was significantly quenched and the FL emission at 543 nm was negligibly changed. The electron transfer (ET) between COO− of N-CDs and Hg2+ led to the FL quenching of N-CDs. The FL ratio (F436/F543) exhibited a good linear relationship in the Hg2+ concentrations of 1161.51 μM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of ~37 nM. The sensor presented high selectivity, satisfactory accuracy and precision toward Hg2+ in seafood with recoveries of 86.45–114.47% and RSDs of 0.20–1.92%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Food safety
Ratiometric sensor
Fluorescent sensor
Heavy metal
Food contamination
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric FL detection of Hg2+ in seafood samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of N-CDs (the inset is the particle size distribution histogram); (B) FTIR spectra of N-CDs, DPA, Tb-DPA, N-CDs-Tb-DPA and N-CDs-Tb-DPA + Hg2+; (C) XPS spectrum of N-CDs-Tb-DPA; (D) High resolution XPS spectra of N1s in N-CDs-Tb-DPA and N-CDs-Tb-DPA + Hg2+; (E) High resolution XPS spectrum of O1s in N-CDs-Tb-DPA and N-CDs-Tb-DPA + Hg2+; (F) UV–vis absorption (purple line), excitation (pink line), and emission (dark cyan line) spectra of N-CDs-Tb-DPA (the insets of (a) and (b) are before and after excited with 365 nm UV lamp). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) Quenching efficiency (Effq (%)) of N-CDs-Hg2+at different pH; (B) Incubation time of N-CDs-Hg2+ v.s. FL intensity; (C) Incubation temperature of N-CDs-Tb-DPA-Hg2+ v.s. FL intensity.

(A) FL emission spectra of N-CDs-Tb-DPA (λex = 290 nm) upon addition of Hg2+ with different concentrations; (B) The plot of the fluorescence intensity ratio (F436/F543) v.s. concentration of Hg2+ (Inset: a linear region).

(A) F436/F543 of 36.49 μM Hg2+ and Zn2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Ag+ and Cu2+ (3 equiv.) and Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+ and I− (10 equiv.), respectively; (B) F436/F543 of 36.49 μM Hg2+ and different co-existing metal ions of Zn2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Ag+ and Cu2+ (3 equiv.) and Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+ and I− (10 equiv.). Different lowercase letters in the histograms represent significant difference (P < 0.05, n = 3).
Schematic illustration of the N-CDs-Tb-DPA sensor for detection of Hg2+.
Recoveries (mean ± SD) for the detection of Hg2+ in seafood samples.

head.ce:title: Terbium (III)-referenced N-doped carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescent sensing of mercury (II) in seafood

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescence (FL) sensor was fabricated by coordinating 2, 6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) sensitized Tb3+ (Tb-DPA) with NH2 and COOH on the surface of the N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for detecting Hg2+ in seafood. The sensor exhibited two FL emissions at 436 nm (N-CDs) as the response signal and at 543 nm (Tb-DPA) as the reference signal when excited at 290 nm. After adding Hg2+, the FL emission at 436 nm was significantly quenched and the FL emission at 543 nm was negligibly changed. The electron transfer (ET) between COO− of N-CDs and Hg2+ led to the FL quenching of N-CDs. The FL ratio (F436/F543) exhibited a good linear relationship in the Hg2+ concentrations of 1161.51 μM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of ~37 nM. The sensor presented high selectivity, satisfactory accuracy and precision toward Hg2+ in seafood with recoveries of 86.45–114.47% and RSDs of 0.20–1.92%.

ce:keywords.ce:section-title: Chemical compounds studied in this article

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury (Hg) contamination in ocean mainly derives from the discharge of metal-containing waste of industrial processes, transfer of riverine or atmospheric and erosion of coast and seafloor (Stankovic & Jovic, 2012). Hg can be accumulated in marine plants and animals due to the contamination of the environment where marine species live. The organisms at the top of the food chain may present higher accumulated Hg concentrations than those in lower trophic levels. This trophic biomagnification implies the special pathway for the bioaccumulation of Hg by seafood with potential risks to human health (Rodrigues, Ferrari, dos Santos, & Conte, 2019). As a virulent heavy metal, Hg can cause motor and cognitive dysfunction, renal impairment and cardiovascular diseases through the accumulation by human body even at trace level (Gao, Song et al., 2018; Gao, Ma et al., 2018). The maximum level (ML) of Hg in salt food grade has been enacted at 0.1 mg/kg by FAO/WHO (2016). Therefore, monitoring Hg residues in seafood have utmost significance but largely remain a challenge. Conventional analytical methods such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (Caylak et al., 2019), cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry (CV-AFS) (Yun, He, Wang, Wang, & Jiang, 2013), inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Krishna, Chandrasekaran, & Karunasagar, 2010) and electrochemical method have been applied for detecting Hg2+ (Chen et al., 2014). These methods are limited by rigorous analytical procedures, high cost, complicated sample preparation and insufficient selectivity in complex food matrix. Hence, it is still a major challenge to develop facile, convenient and high sensitive and selective methods for Hg2+ determination to prevent the potential risk of Hg2+ to human health.

Recently, fluorescence (FL) techniques have attracted tremendous attention for detecting Hg2+ owing to their advantages of real-time analysis, simplicity, and cost-effectiveness (Luo et al., 2018). FL of these methods usually derives from luminescent nanomaterials such as quantum dots (QDs), metallic nanoclusters, graphene oxides, organic dyes and carbon dots (CDs). Compared to the conventional QDs, CDs hold great promise in imaging or metal ions detecting in organism samples due to their fascinating merits of excellent photostability, good biocompatibility, low toxicity and facile synthesis (Xiang et al., 2018), which facilitates their development as biosensors and chemical sensors for biological labeling, drug delivery, optronics, photocatalysis and analysis (Algarra et al., 2014). However, CDs based FL sensors often suffer from low FL quantum yields (FLQYs), which significantly restricts their practical applications (Moniruzzaman & Kim, 2019). Nitrogen doping can be employed to regulate the electronic structure of CDs by providing a lone pair of electrons and neutralizing the distribution of charge, which can enhance the affinity between CDs and targets by supplying abundant active sites. In addition, the introduction of nitrogen can change the surface state of CDs and thus offer energy traps for the FL enhancement of CDs. As a transition metal with strong electron-donating ability, Hg2+ can be captured by oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g. COOH and OH) of CDs and thus change the surface state of CDs, leading to FL quenching of CDs (Han et al., 2019; Shiravand, Badiei, & Mohammadi Ziarani, 2017). This will provide a convenient and economical platform for the detection of Hg2+ by observing the changes of FL intensity.

Nevertheless, single FL CDs based sensors toward targets in complex matrix usually encounter with strong interferences by the matrix substances and the microenvironment around sensors (Gui et al., 2019). Ratiometric FL sensors are considered to be ideal alternatives, which can tackle the inherent weakness of the single FL sensors by measuring the ratio of dual-emission intensities at different wavelengths with a single excitation. Ideally, ratiometric FL sensors are similar to the internal calibration in analysis and therefore possess a high signal-to-noise ratio, which is more suitable for highly sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ in complex matrix (Jiang et al., 2019). Two approaches are often used to construct ratiometric FL sensors. One is to introduce two materials with a response signal and a reference signal. The reference signal is target-insensitive and has a negligible FL change triggered by target comparing to the sensitive and specific response signal to the same target. Another approach is to employ two target-responsive signals with different intensity changes to obtain dual-emission ratiometric FL sensors (Gui et al., 2019). The FL intensity ratio of the response signal and the reference signal (denoted as Fresponse/Freference) is used to improve the accuracy, anti-interference, and reproducibility of target detection. Ratiometric FL sensors can be fabricated by embedding, self-assembling or conjugating one FL material with other fluorophore. Most of these methods require sophisticated design and operations. Ratiometric FL sensors can be facilely fabricated via directly chemical conjugating of CDs with other fluorophores by utilizing multiple active sites on the surface of CDs. As a substitute for conventional organic chromophores, Lanthanide (Ln3+) luminescence presents attractive due to their advantages of larger Stokes shift, narrower emission peak, longer FL life, and higher chemical and photochemical stability (Eliseeva & Bunzli, 2010). The luminescence properties of Ln3+ are mainly derived from 4f-4f and 4f-5d electron transitions. Since the outer layer of electron-filled 5s25p6 has a shielding effect on the inner 4f electrons, the energy gap involved in the 4f-4f electron transition of Ln3+ is fixed. So the emission wavelength and emission color of Ln3+, typically Dy3+, Eu3+ and Tb3+, hardly vary with the matrix substances (Yang et al., 2008). However, aromatic chromophores functioning as antennae are demanded for efficient excitation of metal-based luminescence after intersystem crossing due to the Laporte-forbidden nature of the f-f transitions (Cosby, Ahn, & Boros, 2018). Tb3+ with the unique characteristic of Ln3+ can emit green FL after receiving energy from the antenna. As the antenna of Tb3+, 2, 6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) can offer its O atoms of COOH and N atom of the aromatic ring for the coordination of Tb3+ and can thus transfer its absorbed energy to Tb3+ after intersystem crossing to sensitize the green FL of Tb3+ (Liu et al., 2019; Tan, Liu, & Chen, 2012). Since the antenna excitation wavelength and the lanthanide emission wavelength usually present large Stokes shift, the ratiometric FL sensor can be constructed with DPA sensitized Tb3+ as the reference signal and N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as the response signal when excited by a single wavelength, providing a novel FL sensor for highly selective detection of Hg2+ in food matrix.

In this work, a ratiometric FL sensor based on terbium (III)-referenced N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs-Tb-DPA) is achieved for the determination of Hg2+ in seafood (Scheme 1 ). N-CDs are synthesized by the one-pot hydrothermal method using citric acid and urea as precursors. The excellent sensitivity of N-CDs is derived from the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface which can be employed to capture Hg2+. The O atom of COOH and N atom of NH2 of N-CDs can directly coordinate with Tb atom in Tb-DPA at room temperature to form the N-CDs-Tb-DPA sensor (Chen et al., 2015). The as-prepared ratiometric FL sensor exhibits two characteristic emissions at 436 nm of N-CDs as the response signal and at 543 nm of Tb-DPA as the reference signal when excited at 290 nm. The ratiometric FL approach exhibits stronger anti-interference and satisfactory accuracy and precision for quantitative detecting Hg2+ in seafood.

Citric acid (CA), NaOH, KCl, HgCl2, CaCl2, AgCl, PbCl2, KI, ZnCl2 and NaCl were purchased from Guangdong Guanghua sci-tech Co., Ltd (Guangdong, China). Urea, acetic acid, MgCl2, MnCl2 were obtained from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). Quinine sulfate, CuCl2·2H2O, TbCl3·6H2O and 2, 6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) were bought from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). H3PO4, H2SO4 and HNO3 were acquired from Xilong Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Guangdong, China). CrCl3 and AlCl3 were purchased from Tianjin Bodi Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer of various pH values were prepared with different ratios between NaOH (0.2 M) and the mixture of acetic acid, H2SO4 and HNO3. All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and were used without further purification. The seafood samples, including prawn (penneropenaeus chinensis), seaweed (saccharina japonica), octopus (octopodidae) and large yellow croaker (larimichthys crocea), were obtained from a local supermarket. All solutions were prepared using deionized water (DW) in experimental procedures.

The morphology of N-CDs was observed by JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra was obtained on a Vetex70 FTIR Spectroscope (Bruker Corp, Germany) by using KBr pellets with a scanning speed of 4 cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by a K-Alpha XPS (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained with a UV–vis 2550 Spectrophotometer (Japan). The fluorescence spectra were collected on a quartz cell with 10 mm path length from 200 to 600 nm using a Perkin-Elmer LS-55 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer at room temperature (Maryland, USA).

N-CDs was prepared by the one-pot hydrothermal synthesis. Briefly, 4.2 g citric acid and 6.0 g urea were added to 40 mL of DW by ultra-sonication. Then the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined steel autoclave (100 mL) and heated to 180 °C for 4 h. The resultant solution was naturally cooled down to room temperature. The obtained N-CDs dark green suspension was filtered through a 0.45 μm polyether sulfone (PES) membrane and was stored at room temperature for further use.

N-CDs-Tb-DPA nanocomposites were fabricated via directly chemical conjugating of N-CDs with Tb-DPA. Firstly, 10 mL DPA aqueous solution (10 mM) was mixed with 0.02 g TbCl3·6H2O to form a clear solution. Then the solution was added into 54 mL N-CDs (1 mg/mL) and was stirred overnight at room temperature. Then the resultant solution was dialyzed through a 500–1000 kD membrane for 48 h. Finally, the N-CDs-Tb-DPA was collected for the next experiment.

In order to improve the sensitivity and selectivity of the N-CDs-Tb-DPA sensor for detecting Hg2+, the detection system pH (2.0–9.0), the incubation time of N-CDs + Hg2+ (0–500 s) and the incubation temperature of N-CDs-Tb-DPA + Hg2+ (15–40 °C) were optimized. Since the FL intensity of N-CDs was used as the response signal, the effect of the detection system pH on the FL quenching efficiency (Effq, Eq. (1)) of N-CDs was investigated. Under the optimum conditions, 640 μL (9 μg/mL) of N-CDs-Tb-DPA solution and gradient concentrations of Hg2+ standard solutions were added into 2 mL Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer (under the optimal detection system pH, diluted by 1000 times), then the mixture was subjected to FL measurements after the optimal incubation time at the optimal temperature. All FL measurements were performed in triplicate.
(1)
Ef
f q
%
=
F 0
- F
/
F 0
where F and F0 represented the FL intensity of N-CDs at 436 nm in the presence and absence of Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric FL detection of Hg2+ in seafood samples
ce:section.ce:para: Seafood samples were pretreated with a digestion procedure (Lan, Niu, & Li, 2018; Voegborlo & Adimado, 2010). Each sample (0.5 g) was put in a conical flask, and then 15 mL HNO3 and 2.5 mL H2SO4 were dripped in turn. The mixture was stood overnight at room temperature, and then was boiled until the solution became clear. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was adjusted to pH ~6.0 with 1 M NaOH solution. The resultant solution was diluted to 100 mL with DW. Finally, seafood samples spiked with standard solutions of Hg2+ at concentrations of 18.24, 22.81, and 30.40 μM were used to evaluate the applicability of N-CDs-Tb-DPA for detecting Hg2+ in real samples. All FL measurements were performed in triplicate.

Statistical analysis was calculated using Minitab 16.0 software (Minitab Inc., USA). The one-way ANOVA and Tukey-HSD were conducted to determine significant difference among groups. All FL measurements were performed in triplicate.

The morphology of N-CDs was investigated by TEM, and the image was shown in Fig. 1 A. The obtained N-CDs were spherical and uniformly dispersed. The particle size of N-CDs was 0.84–7.50 nm with an average diameter of 4.48 nm (insets of Fig. 1A), which was consisted with the concept of CDs within the small particle size of 10 nm (Gao, Song et al., 2018; Gao, Ma et al., 2018). Besides, significantly difference of FL intensity can be observed between N-CDs and CDs at the same concentration of 5 μg/mL when excited at 290 nm (Fig. S1A, P < 0.01). As seen from Fig. S1B, N-CDs had a higher quantum yield (QY) (λex = 360 nm, quinine sulfate reference) of 32.74% than that of 18.34% of CDs because N-doping introduced a new surface state labeled as the N-state (Dong et al., 2013; Shen, Shang, Chen, Wang, & Cai, 2017). FTIR spectra of N-CDs, DPA, Tb-DPA and N-CDs-Tb-DPA were presented in Fig. 1B. Compared with the FTIR spectrum of DPA, the peak shifts of DPA-Tb in the symmetrical stretching vibration of CO from 1382 cm−1 to 1396 cm−1 and the CN stretching vibrations of aromatic ring from 750 cm−1 to 767 cm−1 revealed that Tb3+ was chelated to DPA through coordination with N atom of pyridine ring and O atoms of carboxylic acid groups (Tan et al., 2012). The new broad absorption peak of NH at 3136 cm−1 in DPA-Tb was resulted from the protonation of the lone pair of pyridine nitrogen. As for the FTIR spectra of N-CDs, Tb-DPA and N-CDs-Tb-DPA, the absorption peaks at 3417, 1614, and 1072 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of OH/NH (Tang et al., 2018), the asymmetrical stretching vibration of CO and the bending vibration of CO, respectively. These results confirmed the existence of COOH, NH2 and CONH on the surface of N-CDs-Tb-DPA. These active groups of N-CDs-Tb-DPA endowed N-CDs with higher hydrophilicity in aqueous solution, which might facilitate the coordination of N-CDs with Tb-DPA (Chen et al., 2015). The FTIR spectrum of N-CDs-Tb-DPA showed that the surface groups of N-CDs were still present after the addition of Tb-DPA compared with N-CDs and Tb-DPA. The functional groups and chemical composition of N-CDs-Tb-DPA were investigated by XPS, which was shown in Fig. 1C. Four peaks at 1228.20, 532.20, 400.20, and 285.20 eV respectively represented Tb3d5/2, O1s, C1s and N1s. The high N1s atomic percentage of 13% indicated that N had been successfully doped into CDs. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s could be de-convoluted into three peaks at 284.97, 288.27 and 288.97 eV (Fig. S2), which were attributed to CC/CC, CO and CO groups. The spectrum of O1s contained two peaks of C-OH (532.75 eV) and CO (531.63 eV) (Fig. 1D). The spectrum of N1s de-convoluted into five peaks at 399.70, 400.26, 400.63, 401.04 and 401.69 eV corresponded to pyridine N, C-NH2, pyrrolic N, graphite N and NH (Fig. 1E), indicating that the nitrogenous functional groups such as pyridine, pyrrole and graphite N were formed when N was doped into the aromatic structure of graphite. The FTIR and XPS analyses demonstrated that N-CDs-Tb-DPA was successfully synthesized with the abundant active groups of OH, COOH and NH2, providing potential recognition sites for chelating Hg2+.

The optical properties of N-CDs-Tb-DPA were characterized by UV–vis and FL spectra, which were shown in Fig. 1F. The obtained N-CDs-Tb-DPA exhibited both absorption peaks of Tb-DPA at 270 and 278 nm and N-CDs at 230 and 330 nm. The peaks at 270 and 278 nm were attributed to π-π* and n-π* transitions of pyridine (Shi, Yang, Dong, & Yu, 2018). The absorption peaks at 230 nm and 330 nm of N-CDs were ascribed to π-π* transition of the aromatic CC sp2 domains and n-π* transition of CO or C-OH on the surface of N-CDs (Chen et al., 2015). Fig. 1F also showed that N-CDs-Tb-DPA had an excitation wavelength centered at 290 nm and three emission wavelengths centered at 436 nm, 489 nm and 543 nm. The emission wavelength at 436 nm was caused by the surface state of N-CDs and the other two emission wavelengths at 489 nm and 543 nm were the characteristic transitions of 5D4-7F6 and 5D4-7F5 of Tb3+ (Bhardwaj, Bhardwaj, Mehta, Kim, & Deep, 2016). The insets of Fig. 1F displayed that the diluted N-CDs-Tb-DPA solution was pale green under ambient light (Fig. 1F a) and exhibited intense blue FL under UV light at 365 nm (Fig. 1F b), indicating that N-CDs-Tb-DPA possessed remarkable luminescence. The UV–vis spectra of N-CDs, N-CDs-Tb-DPA and Tb-DPA were shown in Fig. S3A. As shown in Fig. S3A, the UV–vis spectrum of N-CDs-Tb-DPA exhibited a blue-shift at 330 nm compared with N-CDs, displaying the successful interaction of N-CDs and Tb-DPA. These results indicated that the multi-emission fluorescent sensor of N-CDs-Tb-DPA was successfully fabricated, which paved the way to further construct the ratiometric FL sensor. The influence of the excitation wavelength on emission intensity was shown in Fig. S4. The FL emission of the N-CDs-Tb-DPA sensor exhibited excitation-independence with emissions at 436 nm, 489 nm and 543 nm when excited at 210–292 nm. In addition, the N-CDs-Tb-DPA sensor possessed a large emission interval of 107 nm between 436 nm and 543 nm, implying its great potential for analytical applications. Thus, the suitable excitation wavelength was 290 nm and the corresponding emission wavelengths were 436 and 543 nm. In addition, the FL intensity ratio (F436/F543) of the FL intensity at 436 nm of N-CDs (F436) and at 543 nm of Tb3+ (F543) was determined as the measurement index for the ratiometric FL determination of Hg2+.

The FL quenching efficiency Effq of N-CDs after dripping Hg2+ at different detection system pH of 2.0–9.0 was displayed in Fig. 2 A. Fig. 2A showed that Effq of N-CDs increased rapidly in the pH range of 2.0–5.0, then sequentially increased at a low increment rate within pH 5.0–7.0, and achieved the maximum Effq of 87.93% at pH 7.0 (P < 0.05). With the increase of pH, the protonation of COOH on the surface of N-CDs was slowly replaced by the deprotonation process, leading to the negatively charged COOH maximally capturing the oppositely charged Hg2+ (Luo et al., 2018). Effq of N-CDs decreased with the pH increase in the range of 7.0–9.0 (P < 0.05) because Hg2+ was more easily coordinated by OH− in the detection system than N-CDs. Consequently, pH 7.0 was chosen as the optimal pH for the effective coordination of Hg2+. The influence of incubation time on the FL intensity of N-CDs after dripping Hg2+ was shown in Fig. 2B. The FL intensity significantly decreased with the incubation time of 0–420 s and then leveled to stable values after 420 s. No significant differences were observed in the FL intensity for the incubation time of 420–500 s (P > 0.05), indicating that Hg2+ could be quickly captured by the functional groups of COOH located on N-CDs to quench the FL of N-CDs in 420 s. The influence of the incubation temperature of 15–40 °C on the N-CDs-Tb-DPA sensor after adding Hg2+ was also studied, and the result was shown in Fig. 2C. As seen from Fig. 2C, F436/F543 of N-CDs-Tb-DPA was stable in the temperature of 15–40 °C (P > 0.05), indicating its outstanding temperature adaptation. Hence, the detection system pH of 7.0, the incubation time of 420 s, and the incubation temperature of 15–40 °C were chosen as the optimum parameters for the subsequent experiments to ensure the sensitivity and selectivity of the N-CDs-Tb-DPA sensor for detecting Hg2+. Furthermore, the N-CDs-Tb-DPA sensor exhibited good photostability when stored at room temperature for one month (P > 0.05, Fig. S5). The N-CDs-Tb-DPA sensor with excellent stability possessed potential for practical sensing applications.

Under the optimal conditions (i.e. detection system pH of 7.0, incubation time of 420 s, and the chosen incubation temperature of 25 °C from 15 to 40 °C), 640 μL of N-CDs-Tb-DPA (9 μg/mL), 2 mL of BR buffer (pH = 7.0), different concentrations of Hg2+ (0–364 μM) were successively dripped into centrifuge tubes. The FL spectra were scanned after incubating at 25 °C for 420 s, which were shown in Fig. 3 A. The FL at 436 nm was dramatically quenched with increasing Hg2+ concentration from 0 to 364 μM while an unobvious signal change at 543 nm was observed. The calculated F436/F543 in the Hg2+ concentration of 0–364 μM was shown in Fig. 3B. A good linear relationship between F436/F543 and Hg2+ concentrations of 1–161.51 μM was obtained. The calibration equation was described as F436/F543 = −0.043X + 6.959 with the coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.996 (X is the concentration of Hg2+, μM). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be ~37 nM according to Eq. (2). Comparing with other analytical methods for determining Hg2+ (Table S1), the as-prepared N-CDs-Tb-DPA sensor presented obvious advantages such as lower detection limit and wider linear range.
(2)
LOD = 3 S b / K s v
where Sb is the standard deviation of the blank sample (n = 8) and Ksv is the slope of the calibration curve (Wang et al., 2016).

High selectivity is an important index for the sensor in real sample detections. The selectivity of the sensor was evaluated by separately adding different metal ions to the N-CDs-Tb-DPA sensor, and the results were shown in Fig. 4 A and Fig. S6. As seen from Fig. 4A and Fig. S6, F436/F543 decreased significantly by adding Hg2+ of 36.49 μM, while F436/F543 presented unobvious inhibition by adding Zn2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Ag+ and Cu2+ with 3-fold concentration of Hg2+ or Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+ and I− with 10-fold concentration of Hg2+. The values of F436/F543 showed significantly different between Hg2+ and different ions (P < 0.05), indicating the high selectivity of the sensor toward Hg2+. In addition, the anti-interference ability of the sensor to the coexisting ions was verified through competitive experiments. Zn2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Ag+ and Cu2+ with 3-fold concentration of Hg2+ and Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+ and I− with 10-fold concentration of Hg2+ were separately mixed with Hg2+ of 36.49 μM. No significant differences were observed in the resultant values of F436/F543 with/without these co-existing ions (Fig. 4B, P > 0.05). These observations indicated that the N-CDs-Tb-DPA sensor possessed outstanding selectivity and anti-interference ability for the determination of Hg2+, which realized its practical applications in complex food matrix.

To investigate the applicability of the proposed sensor in real samples, the N-CDs-Tb-DPA sensor was employed to detect Hg2+ in seafood (prawn, seaweed, octopus and large yellow croaker). Different concentrations of Hg2+ were added into the digested seafood samples and were then detected by the N-CDs-Tb-DPA sensor, and the results were listed in Table 1 . As shown in Table 1, the recoveries of 86.45–114.47% with low relative standard deviations (RSD, n = 3) of 0.20–1.92% were achieved, demonstrating that the N-CDs-Tb-DPA sensor for Hg2+ detection in real seafood samples was highly reliable and feasible with satisfactory accuracy and precision.

The impact of Hg2+ on the FL intensity of the N-CDs-Tb-DPA sensor was presented in Fig. S7A. The FL of N-CDs at 436 nm was remarkably quenched with the quenching ration of ~83.45% and the FL of Tb-DPA at 543 nm only changed slightly with increasing concentrations of Hg2+. As seen from Fig. S7B, the blue FL of N-CDs was significantly quenched by Hg2+ under UV light of 365 nm while the green FL of Tb3+ in the blue-green FL sensor became gradually obvious under UV light of 254 nm. The FL quenching of N-CDs might be caused by the electron transfer (ET) effect between Hg2+ and the oxygen-containing functional group on the surface of N-CDs, changing the electron cloud density of N which led to the decrease of energy traps (Sun, Hwang, & Xie, 2011).

In order to further explore the quench mechanism, UV–vis, FTIR and XPS were conducted. The UV–vis spectra of N-CDs-Tb-DPA and N-CDs-Tb-DPA + Hg2+ were shown in Fig. S2B. Compared with the UV–vis spectrum of N-CDs-Tb-DPA, the blue shift of N-CDs-Tb-DPA + Hg2+ was attributed to the formation of complex between N-CDs-Tb-DPA and Hg2+. The FTIR spectrum of N-CDs-Tb-DPA + Hg2+ (refer to Fig. 1B) displayed that the multiple functional groups on the surface of the sensor still existed after the addition of Hg2+ compared with N-CDs-Tb-DPA. Furthermore, N-CDs-Tb-DPA + Hg2+ was analyzed by XPS to further confirm the recognition and the quenching mechanism between the N-CDs-Tb-DPA sensor and Hg2+ (refer to Fig. 1C). The XPS spectrum indicated that the five peaks centered at 1254.17, 537.17, 404.67, 285.01 and 100.33 eV could be respectively attributed to Tb3d5, O1s, C1s, N1s and Hg4f. Hg4f could be de-convoluted into three peaks at 100.00, 100.89 and 104.94 eV which were respectively assigned to Hg, Hg-O (Hg 4f7/2) and Hg-O (Hg 4f5/2) (Fig. S7C), indicating that Hg2+ binding to the sensor was mainly in oxidation state. The introduction of N (electron donors) into CDs can effectively harmonize the electronic densities of N-CDs to form a new surface state, which facilitated the coordination interaction between COOH and Hg2+ (Zhu et al., 2013). The functional group of COOH on the surface of N-CDs, which was liable to lose its electrons and became ionized carboxylic groups (COO−), could strongly interact with Hg2+ and accelerate the electron transfer process of Hg2+ oxidation, leading to the formation of carbon-mercury compound. And the carbon-mercury compounds were decomposed into Hg-O due to its instability (Luo et al., 2019). The spectrum of O1s was de-convoluted into two peaks at 533.07 eV (C-OH) and 531.47 eV (CO) (refer to Fig. 1D), indicating that the binding energy of C-OH was enhanced from 532.75 eV to 533.07 eV. Hg2+ as an electron donor presented electron shielding effect on the oxygen of COO−, thus the C-OH bond reduced its electron shielding effect and enhanced its binding energy to neutralize electron density. The spectrum of N1s was de-convoluted into four peaks at 399.67, 400.26, 400.57, 401.17 and 401.85 eV, corresponding to Pyridine N, C-NH2, Pyrrolic N, Graphite N and NH, respectively (refer to Fig. 1E). The enhanced binding energy of NH from 401.69 eV to 401.85 eV indicated that the electron shielding effect of NH was weakened. The higher capability of electron donation of Hg2+ than NH2 inhibited ET between NH2 and COOH, decreasing the electron shielding effect of NH and quenching the FL of N-CDs by changing the electron density of N. Thus Hg2+ bonded on the sensor was mainly in oxidation state and COOH was the main active site to chelate Hg2+. Moreover, the XPS spectra of N-CDs and N-CDs + Hg2+ were also recorded to explain the mechanism of FL quenching of N-CDs. As for N-CDs + Hg2+, the XPS spectrum of Hg 4f could be de-convoluted into two peaks at 101.17 eV and 105.07 eV which were assigned to Hg-O (Hg 4f7/2) and Hg-O (Hg 4f5/2) (Fig. S7D). These results of oxidation state of Hg were in consist with the proposed hypothesis above. In summary, Hg2+ could be chelated by COO− on the surface of N-CDs, which accelerated the non-radioactive recombination of excitons via effective ET and induced the change of surface state, leading to the efficient FL quenching of N-CDs (Hou, Lu, Deng, Li, & Zhang, 2015). The splendid selectivity and sensitively of the N-CDs-Tb-DPA sensor was attributed to the stronger affinity of the COO−-rich N-CDs toward Hg2+ than other metal ions and the unique quenching process caused by Hg2+ (Chai et al., 2010).

We reported a ratiometric fluorescence (FL) sensor, which was facilely assembled by coordinating Tb-DPA and N-CDs at room temperature, for sensitively and selectively detecting Hg2+ in seafood. The FL intensity at 436 nm of N-CDs was remarkably quenched while the FL intensity at 543 nm of Tb-DPA was slightly changed after the addition of Hg2+ when excited at 290 nm. Hg2+ was effectively captured by the abundant COO− on the surface of N-CDs and the binding to the sensor was mainly in oxidation state. The FL quenching of N-CDs-Tb-DPA + Hg2+ was ascribed to the higher capability of electron donation of Hg2+ than NH2, which inhibited ET between NH2 and COOH, leading to the change of electron density of N. The unique quenching caused by Hg2+ endowed the sensor with excellent selectivity, satisfactory accuracy and precision for the determination of Hg2+ in seafood, which paved a new avenue for the design of novel ratiometric FL sensors for potential applications in food safety.

Xie He: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing - original draft. Yong Han: Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Xueli Luo: Software, Supervision. Weixia Yang: Software, Supervision. Chunhua Li: Resources, Supervision. Wenzhi Tang: Resources, Supervision. Tianli Yue: Resources, Supervision. Zhonghong Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Social Development Grant of Shaanxi Province, China (2018SF-401).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.126624.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
